title: AMD teams up with Eric Trump-backed Foundation to build humanoid robots
excerpt: AMD has spent the year trying to prove it can do more than trail Nvidia. Its latest move takes the fight somewhere new, to the inside of a humanoid robot. The chipmaker has agreed to supply the compute for machines built by Foundation, a San Francisco startup whose backers include Eric Trump. For a company […]
content_html:
AMD has spent the year trying to prove it can do more than trail Nvidia. Its latest move takes the fight somewhere new, to the inside of a humanoid robot.

The chipmaker has agreed to supply the compute for machines built by Foundation, a San Francisco startup whose backers include Eric Trump. For a company long cast as the perennial number two in AI silicon, the choice of battleground is pointed.

The company is not a conventional robotics outfit. Its Phantom machines have been tested in Ukraine, and Foundation has since won Pentagon contracts to develop humanoid robots for the US military. Heavy industry and the battlefield are, in its telling, the same market.

Under the deal, announced on July 23, Foundation’s robots will run on AMD’s Ryzen AI Embedded X100 Series processors, the embedded chips the company introduced in January. Financial terms were not disclosed.

Eric Trump has been an investor in Foundation since early 2026 and serves as its chief strategy adviser. He and his brother Donald Trump Jr. have backed a string of defence and hardware firms, among them the Israeli drone-maker XTEND, Unusual Machines, and Powerus.

The 💜 of EU tech

The latest rumblings from the EU tech scene, a story from our wise ol' founder Boris, and some questionable AI art. It's free, every week, in your inbox. Sign up now!

The pattern has drawn scrutiny over potential conflicts of interest, given how often such firms compete for government work while a family member sits in the White House.

Foundation’s defence business has moved quickly regardless, from battlefield trials to procurement talks in a matter of months.

Foundation says its first-generation Phantom MK-1 robots helped build more than 24,000 cars in 2025 and now run five days a week inside customer deployments, a level of uptime few humanoid makers can yet claim.

It puts its contracted annual recurring revenue, the figure it has signed rather than banked, at $100 million.

Leasing an industrial unit costs roughly $100,000 a year, while a defence version, fitted for materials handling and reconnaissance, runs to $300,000 apiece.

The next model, the Phantom MK-2, has been engineered around AMD silicon from the start, hardened for factory floors and combat alike.

Foundation says the machine tolerates 100G of vibration, carries an IP67 dust and water rating, and sees in 360 degrees, the kind of specification sheet written for a warehouse and a war zone in equal measure.

It also describes itself as the only company building dual-use humanoids for both heavy industry and defence, a claim that is difficult to test against a field of rivals making similar noises.

Much of the appeal lies in the hands. AMD’s programmable FPGA chips, inherited from its Xilinx acquisition, let Foundation coordinate 23 degrees of freedom while folding in high-frequency tactile feedback, according to the company’s Andrea Esposito, in “a single deterministic control loop”.

The broader deal pulls in AMD’s CPUs, graphics, and adaptive computing to run perception, reasoning, and motor control on the same platform.

For AMD, the prize is a foothold in a market Nvidia has quietly made its own through its Jetson edge modules and Isaac simulation software, which a long list of robotics startups already lean on.

“Advanced humanoid robots require tightly coordinated compute across perception, reasoning and real-time control,” said Salil Raje, an AMD senior vice-president.

The company claims its chip delivers 2.5 times faster inference and 3 times faster training than Nvidia’s, though it has not published the benchmarks, the methodology, or the rival parts behind those figures.

The wager is on scale. Foundation plans to open a factory in October capable of building 5,000 robots a year, with a second site planned for early next year and designed for 50,000.

Neither company disclosed how many chips the deal covers or what it is worth. Money is pouring into the field elsewhere, from a $1.4 billion round for Germany’s NEURA Robotics to a crowded pack of Chinese manufacturers now facing a commercialisation reckoning.

Whether AMD has bet on the right robot, or merely the best-connected one, will take longer to settle.

The chipmaker has agreed to supply the compute for machines built by Foundation, a San Francisco startup whose backers include Eric Trump. For a company long cast as the perennial number two in AI silicon, the choice of battleground is pointed. The company is not a conventional robotics outfit. Its Phantom machines have been tested in Ukraine, and Foundation has since won Pentagon contracts to develop humanoid robots for the US military. Heavy industry and the battlefield are, in its telling, the same market. Under the deal, announced on July 23, Foundation’s robots will run on AMD’s Ryzen AI Embedded X100 Series processors, the embedded chips the company introduced in January. Financial terms were not disclosed. Eric Trump has been an investor in Foundation since early 2026 and serves as its chief strategy adviser. He and his brother Donald Trump Jr. have backed a string of defence and hardware firms, among them the Israeli drone-maker XTEND, Unusual Machines, and Powerus.The 💜 of EU techThe latest rumblings from the EU tech scene, a story from our wise ol' founder Boris, and some questionable AI art. The pattern has drawn scrutiny over potential conflicts of interest, given how often such firms compete for government work while a family member sits in the White House. Foundation’s defence business has moved quickly regardless, from battlefield trials to procurement talks in a matter of months. Foundation says its first-generation Phantom MK-1 robots helped build more than 24,000 cars in 2025 and now run five days a week inside customer deployments, a level of uptime few humanoid makers can yet claim. It puts its contracted annual recurring revenue, the figure it has signed rather than banked, at $100 million. Leasing an industrial unit costs roughly $100,000 a year, while a defence version, fitted for materials handling and reconnaissance, runs to $300,000 apiece. The next model, the Phantom MK-2, has been engineered around AMD silicon from the start, hardened for factory floors and combat alike. Foundation says the machine tolerates 100G of vibration, carries an IP67 dust and water rating, and sees in 360 degrees, the kind of specification sheet written for a warehouse and a war zone in equal measure. It also describes itself as the only company building dual-use humanoids for both heavy industry and defence, a claim that is difficult to test against a field of rivals making similar noises. Much of the appeal lies in the hands. AMD’s programmable FPGA chips, inherited from its Xilinx acquisition, let Foundation coordinate 23 degrees of freedom while folding in high-frequency tactile feedback, according to the company’s Andrea Esposito, in “a single deterministic control loop”. The broader deal pulls in AMD’s CPUs, graphics, and adaptive computing to run perception, reasoning, and motor control on the same platform. For AMD, the prize is a foothold in a market Nvidia has quietly made its own through its Jetson edge modules and Isaac simulation software, which a long list of robotics startups already lean on. “Advanced humanoid robots require tightly coordinated compute across perception, reasoning and real-time control,” said Salil Raje, an AMD senior vice-president. The company claims its chip delivers 2.5 times faster inference and 3 times faster training than Nvidia’s, though it has not published the benchmarks, the methodology, or the rival parts behind those figures. The wager is on scale. Foundation plans to open a factory in October capable of building 5,000 robots a year, with a second site planned for early next year and designed for 50,000. Neither company disclosed how many chips the deal covers or what it is worth. Money is pouring into the field elsewhere, from a $1.4 billion round for Germany’s NEURA Robotics to a crowded pack of Chinese manufacturers now facing a commercialisation reckoning. Whether AMD has bet on the right robot, or merely the best-connected one, will take longer to settle.

sentences.en: For AMD, the prize is a foothold in a market Nvidia has quietly made its own through its Jetson edge modules and Isaac simulation software, which a long list of robotics startups already lean on.
sentences.zh: 对于 AMD 来说，这个奖项是在英伟达通过其 Jetson 边缘模块和 Isaac 模拟软件悄悄占据市场的一个立足点，一长串机器人初创公司已经依赖这些软件。

sentences.en: “Advanced humanoid robots require tightly coordinated compute across perception, reasoning and real-time control,” said Salil Raje, an AMD senior vice-president.
sentences.zh: AMD 高级副总裁 Salil Raje 表示：“先进的类人机器人需要在感知、推理和实时控制方面进行紧密协调的计算。”

blocks.html_en: The company claims its chip delivers 2.5 times faster inference and 3 times faster training than Nvidia’s, though it has not published the benchmarks, the methodology, or the rival parts behind those figures.

sentences.en: The company claims its chip delivers 2.5 times faster inference and 3 times faster training than Nvidia’s, though it has not published the benchmarks, the methodology, or the rival parts behind those figures.
sentences.zh: 该公司声称其芯片的推理速度比 Nvidia 的快 2.5 倍，训练速度快 3 倍，不过该公司尚未公布这些数据背后的基准、方法或竞争对手的部分。

blocks.html_en: The wager is on scale. Foundation plans to open a factory in October capable of building 5,000 robots a year, with a second site planned for early next year and designed for 50,000.

sentences.en: The wager is on scale.
sentences.zh: 赌注是有规模的。

sentences.en: Foundation plans to open a factory in October capable of building 5,000 robots a year, with a second site planned for early next year and designed for 50,000.
sentences.zh: 基金会计划于 10 月开设一家工厂，每年可生产 5,000 台机器人，第二个工厂计划于明年初建成，设计产能为 50,000 台。

blocks.html_en: Neither company disclosed how many chips the deal covers or what it is worth. Money is pouring into the field elsewhere, from a $1.4 billion round for Germany’s NEURA Robotics to a crowded pack of Chinese manufacturers now facing a commercialisation reckoning.

sentences.en: Neither company disclosed how many chips the deal covers or what it is worth.
sentences.zh: 两家公司均未透露该交易涉及多少芯片及其价值。

sentences.en: Money is pouring into the field elsewhere, from a $1.4 billion round for Germany’s NEURA Robotics to a crowded pack of Chinese manufacturers now facing a commercialisation reckoning.
sentences.zh: 资金正在涌入其他领域，从德国 NEURA Robotics 的 14 亿美元融资，到目前面临商业化清算的众多中国制造商。

blocks.html_en: Whether AMD has bet on the right robot, or merely the best-connected one, will take longer to settle.

sentences.en: Whether AMD has bet on the right robot, or merely the best-connected one, will take longer to settle.
sentences.zh: AMD 是否押注于正确的机器人，或者仅仅是连接最好的机器人，将需要更长的时间才能解决。

content_html_zh:
AMD 花了一年的时间试图证明自己能做的不仅仅是落后于 Nvidia。 它的最新举动将战斗带到了新的地方，即人形机器人的内部。

这家芯片制造商已同意为 Foundation 制造的机器提供计算能力，Foundation 是一家旧金山初创公司，其支持者包括埃里克·特朗普 (Eric Trump)。 对于一家长期被视为人工智能芯片领域排名第二的公司来说，战场的选择是有针对性的。

该公司不是一家传统的机器人公司。 其 Phantom 机器已在乌克兰进行了测试，此后基金会赢得了五角大楼为美军开发人形机器人的合同。 从本质上讲，重工业和战场是同一个市场。

根据 7 月 23 日宣布的协议，Foundation 的机器人将在 AMD 的 Ryzen AI Embedded X100 系列处理器上运行，这是该公司于 1 月份推出的嵌入式芯片。 财务条款并未披露。

埃里克·特朗普自 2026 年初以来一直是 Foundation 的投资者，并担任其首席战略顾问。 他和他的兄弟小唐纳德·特朗普支持了一系列国防和硬件公司，其中包括以色列无人机制造商 XTEND、Unusual Machines 和 Powerus。

欧盟科技的💜

来自欧盟科技界的最新传言、我们明智的创始人鲍里斯的故事，以及一些值得怀疑的人工智能艺术。 每周都会免费发送到您的收件箱中。 立即注册！

考虑到此类公司在家庭成员入主白宫时争夺政府工作的频率，这种模式引起了对潜在利益冲突的审查。

不管怎样，基金会的国防业务进展迅速，在短短几个月内就从战场试验到采购谈判。

Foundation 表示，其第一代 Phantom MK-1 机器人在 2025 年帮助制造了超过 24,000 辆汽车，现在在客户部署中每周运行五天，很少有人形机器人制造商能够达到这样的正常运行时间水平。

该公司将其合同年度经常性收入（该数字是其签署而非存入银行的数字）定为 1 亿美元。

租赁一个工业单元的费用约为每年 10 万美元，而用于物料搬运和侦察的防御版本则每台费用高达 30 万美元。

下一个型号 Phantom MK-2 从一开始就围绕 AMD 芯片进行设计，并针对工厂车间和战斗进行了强化。

Foundation 表示，该机器可承受 100G 的振动，具有 IP67 防尘防水等级，并可 360 度观看，符合为仓库和战区编写的规格表。

它还将自己描述为唯一一家为重工业和国防制造两用人形机器人的公司，这一说法很难与发出类似声音的竞争对手进行检验。

大部分吸引力掌握在手中。 AMD 的可编程 FPGA 芯片继承自收购 Xilinx，让 Foundation 能够协调 23 个自由度，同时折叠高频触觉反馈，根据该公司的 Andrea Esposito 的说法，在“单个确定性控制循环”中。

更广泛的交易涉及 AMD 的 CPU、显卡和自适应计算，以便在同一平台上运行感知、推理和电机控制。

对于 AMD 来说，这个奖项是在英伟达通过其 Jetson 边缘模块和 Isaac 模拟软件悄悄占据市场的一个立足点，一长串机器人初创公司已经依赖这些软件。

AMD 高级副总裁 Salil Raje 表示：“先进的类人机器人需要在感知、推理和实时控制方面进行紧密协调的计算。”

该公司声称其芯片的推理速度比 Nvidia 的快 2.5 倍，训练速度快 3 倍，不过该公司尚未公布这些数据背后的基准、方法或竞争对手的部分。

赌注是有规模的。 基金会计划于 10 月开设一家工厂，每年可生产 5,000 台机器人，第二个工厂计划于明年初建成，设计产能为 50,000 台。

两家公司均未透露该交易涉及多少芯片及其价值。 资金正在涌入其他领域，从德国 NEURA Robotics 的 14 亿美元融资，到目前面临商业化清算的众多中国制造商。

AMD 是否押注于正确的机器人，或者仅仅是连接最好的机器人，将需要更长的时间才能解决。
